comments:
Summary:
This paper investigates the applicability of adversarial example crafting to malware classification. Using the DREBIN Android dataset and feed-forward neural networks trained on static binary features, the authors adapt a forward-derivative / saliency-map attack to the discrete, functionality-constrained domain of malware: they restrict perturbations to adding features (only ones that can be declared in AndroidManifest.xml) and bound L1 distortion. They report high misclassification rates (roughly 60–85% depending on architecture) and evaluate several mitigations (simple feature reduction, mutual-information-based reduction, distillation, and adversarial re-training), reporting that feature reduction often hurts robustness, distillation helps modestly, and adversarial re-training can improve robustness depending on parameters.

Strengths:
- Addresses an important and timely question: do adversarial-example techniques transfer from vision to security-sensitive domains with discrete and constrained inputs. The question has practical significance.
- Proposes a straightforward and sensible adaptation of forward-derivative saliency methods to binary feature spaces and constrained perturbations, which is non-trivial.
- Uses a large realistic dataset (DREBIN) and reports quantitative misclassification rates and required distortions under concrete constraints (manifest-only additions, L1 ≤ 20).
- Evaluates several defense strategies and documents that some defenses (naive feature reduction) can be counter-productive—an important empirical finding.
- Clear experimental setup and reasonable baselines (variety of network architectures and training malware ratios).

Weaknesses / Limitations:
- The assumption that adding entries to AndroidManifest.xml is safe (i.e., will not affect functionality) is plausible but not validated experimentally. In practice, adding certain permissions or components could change behavior or raise user suspicion; demonstrating actual APK modifications and successful installation/run would strengthen the claim.
- Only static features are considered; dynamic or behavioral features (often used in deployed detectors) are not evaluated. It remains unclear how attacks would transfer to detectors relying on dynamic traces or on hybrid features.
- The perturbation model permits only additions to a limited subset of features (manifest-origin features). While conservative and realistic, it is not shown how many malware samples actually can be modified in this way in real APKs without breaking intended behavior or being conspicuous.
- The work focuses on white-box attacks; black-box scenarios (e.g., remote services) are only discussed via citation. Experiments demonstrating transferability (trained substitutes) or black-box effectiveness would increase impact.
- Hyperparameter tuning is acknowledged to be limited. Some sensitivity analyses (variance across random seeds, statistical significance) are missing; reported rates sometimes vary by architecture and malware ratio without a deep explanation.
- Some defenses (distillation) are shown to help only modestly; the discussion could be deeper about why distillation under-performs relative to vision tasks on this data modality.

Suggestions for improvement / future work:
- Empirically validate that the manifest edits used by the attack can be applied to real APKs and that resulting apps remain functional and installable. If possible, evaluate whether such edits are visible to users or static checkers (e.g., manifest size, permissions added).
- Explore black-box attacks and transferability: craft adversarial samples on substitutes and test against held-out networks; evaluate query-limited scenarios to simulate remote detectors.
- Investigate dynamic and hybrid feature sets to see whether adversarial examples remain effective when detectors use runtime behavior.
- Provide more analysis of why feature reduction increases susceptibility (e.g., per-feature importance distributions) and why distillation helps less in this setting—insights could guide better defenses.
- Consider iterative adversarial re-training (multiple rounds) and adversarial-loss-based training (as discussed) as a defense and report convergence behavior.
- Release code and tooling for reproducing APK modifications and the adversarial crafting pipeline.

Minor comments:
- Some parts of the provided text include formatting artifacts; the final submission should ensure figures/tables are clear and all references are consistent.

Overall recommendation:
This paper makes a meaningful and concrete contribution by demonstrating that adversarial-example techniques extend to malware classification under realistic constraints and by empirically evaluating several defenses. The limitations noted above are important but do not negate the main findings. I recommend acceptance (strong accept / positive).
